learning_outcomes.description: Research trends in corporate finance activities within Australian and/or overseas markets of interest and correctly interpret meanings and trends.
Analyse several aspects that relate to a firm's finance such as capital structure, cost of capital, payout policy, financing decisions, capital raising, merger and acquisition.
Evaluate the impact of various aspects of accounting and legal considerations.
In teams, prepare and present a detailed corporate finance pitch.
Develop professional self-awareness and critical industry skills, such as financial modelling and analysis, research, presentation writing and verbal presentation skills.

assessments.task_description:
Three in-class quizzes , in total, are worth 15 points (3 quizzes x 5 points) and account for 15% of the assessment.

Each quiz will comprise of 5 multiple-choice questions with a question worth 1 point.

Quiz 1 will cover Capital Structure.

Quiz 2 will focus on Event Studies and Capital Raising.

Quiz 3, the last one, will examine M&A and Corporate Governance.

The three quizzes will be held during class and students will be given 30 minutes to answer FIVE questions.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Group Assignment One: An Event Study
assessments.task_description:
This is the first of two team based assessments. The teams will be asked to analyse data pertaining to a corporate event and write a report. The data will be provided by the instructor.

Please note that during the semester you will be required to completed a peer assessment of yourself and peers in terms of your and their contribution to the team assignment. You will complete 2 in total.

TEAM COMPOSITION INFORMATION

Teams will consist of four students.

Students will initially have the opportunity to form their own teams. Please coordinate with potential teammates and ensure that all team members sign up together.

If a team has fewer than four members, the course coordinator reserves the right to assign students who are seeking a team in order to bring the team to full capacity.

Team composition will be the same for both assignments. All teams must be finalized and the team compositions communicated to the course coordinator by the start of Week 3 of the semester. Team composition can be communicated to me via email or during class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit the report on Blackboard by the due date.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This is an individual submission worth 20%.

Students are required to prepare an original editorial article suitable for submission to a leading financial newspaper. The article should be written in the style of a professional opinion piece, presenting a clear and persuasive argument on a contemporary issue relevant to corporate finance issue covered in class. This editorial submission should be between 1,000 and 1,200 words, with the final word count clearly stated at the end of the article. Submissions must be formatted using 12-point Times New Roman font, 1.5-line spacing, and standard 2.54 cm (1-inch) margins. The article should be written for an informed but non-specialist audience and should adopt a concise, engaging, and evidence-based writing style. Students should focus on advancing a single, well-defined argument rather than providing a broad descriptive overview of a topic.

Although editorials are written for a general readership, arguments must be grounded in academic research. Students are expected to draw on relevant scholarly literature from peer-reviewed journals and other credible sources to support their claims, evaluate alternative viewpoints, and strengthen the overall persuasiveness of their argument. Academic sources should be integrated seamlessly into the article rather than presented as a literature review. The essay should cite academic sources, with a minimum of three academic sources is required. Citations and a reference list should be provided in accordance with the APA referencing style, while the body of the editorial should maintain the readability and professional tone expected of a financial newspaper opinion piece.

The topic of the essay will be notified on 21st September and the due date is October 7.

Note:

  1. The word count excludes the reference list but includes all in-text citations, headings, and subheadings.
  2. 1200 words is 3-4 pages for documents that comply with the required formatting stated above.

The completed report is DUE by Week 13 FRIDAY 30th October (3 pm).

learning_activities.topic:
Course Introduction and Recap of Capital Structure.

We will focus on introducing the course and provide a recap of capital structure and the role of taxes.

Background reading is Chapter 15 of Berk and DeMarzo. Additional readings from academic articles and newspapers will be posted to Blackboard.


Learning outcomes: L01

learning_activities.topic:
The Costs of Financial Distress

We will focus on all things pertaining to bankruptcy costs that arise due to firms having debt in its capital structure.

Background reading is Chapter 16 of Berk and DeMarzo. Additional readings from academic articles and newspapers will be posted to Blackboard.
